title: Renal Cell Carcinoma
authors: Robert J. Motzer, Martin H. Voss

items.title: Incidence and Demographics of Renal Cell Carcinoma
items.content: The incidence of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) has been rising, with approximately 81,000 cases annually in the United States. It is more common in males than females, with a male-to-female ratio of 2:1. RCC is uncommon under age 45 and peaks between ages 55 and 75.
keyPoints: Incidence rose to ~81,000 cases annually in the U.S.
RCC is the seventh most common cancer overall in the U.S., sixth in males, ninth in females
Male-to-female ratio is 2:1
Uncommon under age 45, peaks between ages 55 and 75
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · Renal Cell Carcinoma | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Risk Factors and Genetic Associations of Renal Cell Carcinoma
items.content: RCC is associated with several risk factors, including cigarette smoking, obesity, hypertension, and polycystic kidney disease complicated by chronic renal failure. Familial forms include clear cell RCC in von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) syndrome, which carries a 70% lifetime risk of developing RCC.
keyPoints: Risk factors: cigarette smoking, obesity, hypertension
Increased risk with polycystic kidney disease and chronic renal failure
Familial forms include VHL syndrome with ~70% lifetime risk for clear cell RCC
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · Renal Cell Carcinoma | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Clear Cell RCC: Pathogenesis and Therapeutic Targeting
items.content: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the most common subtype, accounting for over 70% of cases. It originates from epithelial cells in the proximal tubules and frequently metastasizes (>80%). The pathogenesis involves early loss of chromosome 3p, leading to inactivation of tumor-suppressor genes such as VHL. This results in upregulation of VEGF signaling through hypoxia-inducible factors (HIF), promoting angiogenesis and growth.
keyPoints: Clear cell RCC accounts for >70% of renal malignancies.
>80% of patients with clear cell RCC develop metastases.
Loss of chromosome 3p is the earliest event, affecting genes like VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, and SETD2.
VHL inactivation leads to upregulation of VEGF signaling, promoting angiogenesis.
Clear cell RCC cells are susceptible to antiangiogenesis therapy.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · PATHOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.title: Genomic Features and Clinical Implications of Papillary RCC
items.content: Papillary renal cell carcinoma (RCC) represents 10-15% of cases. It can be bilateral and multifocal, with distinct molecular features such as activating mutations in the MET oncogene or gain of chromosome 7. These genetic alterations are actionable targets for therapy using MET inhibitors.
keyPoints: Papillary RCC accounts for 10–15% of renal malignancies.
Can be bilateral and multifocal.
Characterized by activating mutations in the MET oncogene or gain of chromosome 7.
MET alterations are actionable via targeted MET inhibitors.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · PATHOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.title: Incidental Detection of Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC) via Imaging
items.content: Renal cell carcinoma is often detected incidentally through radiologic imaging, such as CT or MRI, which are used for evaluating other medical conditions. This incidental detection has led to earlier identification of renal masses and improved 5-year survival rates due to the increased use of nephron-sparing surgery.
keyPoints: Incidental finding on a radiograph is common for RCC.
Use of CT or MRI contributes to early detection.
Improved 5-year survival with early detection and nephron-sparing surgery.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Paraneoplastic Syndromes in Renal Cell Carcinoma
items.content: Renal cell carcinoma can present with paraneoplastic syndromes, including erythrocytosis (about 3% at presentation), hypercalcemia, nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction (Stauffer's syndrome), and acquired dysfibrinogenemia. These syndromes were historically significant in diagnosing RCC before modern imaging.
keyPoints: Erythrocytosis noted in about 3% of patients at presentation.
Paraneoplastic syndromes include hypercalcemia, Stauffer's syndrome, and acquired dysfibrinogenemia.
Historically significant for diagnosing RCC before modern imaging.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Understanding RCC Staging and Survival Rates
items.content: The American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) staging system categorizes renal cell carcinoma (RCC) based on tumor size, local invasion, and metastasis. Stage I tumors are ≤7 cm and confined to the kidney; stage II tumors are >7 cm but still confined to the kidney; stage III tumors extend beyond the kidney into Gerota's fascia or involve regional lymph nodes; stage IV involves distant metastases. The 5-year survival rate for all RCCs is 77%, varying significantly by stage.
keyPoints: Stage I: ≤7 cm, confined to kidney
Stage II: >7 cm, confined to kidney
Stage III: extends beyond kidney into Gerota's fascia or involves regional lymph nodes
Stage IV: distant metastases
65% present with Stage I or II; 15-20% with Stage III or IV
5-year survival rate is 77%, varies by stage
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · STAGING AND PROGNOSIS | LOCALIZED TUMOR

items.title: Management and Prognosis of Localized RCC
items.content: For localized RCC (stages I, II, selected III), the standard treatment is radical or partial nephrectomy. Radical nephrectomy involves removing Gerota's fascia contents, including the kidney, and sometimes the ipsilateral adrenal gland and lymph nodes. Partial nephrectomy is preferred for patients with solitary kidneys, bilateral tumors, or chronic renal insufficiency to preserve kidney function. Adjuvant therapies have shown limited efficacy; however, pembrolizumab improves disease-free survival in high-risk recurrence cases post-nephrectomy.
keyPoints: Radical nephrectomy: en bloc removal of Gerota's fascia contents
Partial nephrectomy: for solitary kidneys, bilateral tumors, or chronic renal insufficiency
Pembrolizumab approved for high-risk recurrence post-nephrectomy
Adjuvant therapies largely ineffective in randomized trials
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · STAGING AND PROGNOSIS | LOCALIZED TUMOR

items.title: Role of Nephrectomy in Stage IV Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC)
items.content: Nephrectomy, or cytoreductive nephrectomy, is considered for carefully selected patients with stage IV RCC. It may be performed to alleviate symptoms such as pain or hemorrhage from the primary tumor.
keyPoints: Cytoreductive nephrectomy can improve long-term survival in some patients with a solitary metastatic site that is resectable.
Surgery has limited role generally for metastatic RCC.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · METASTATIC DISEASE

items.title: Systemic Therapy and Targeted Immunotherapies in Metastatic RCC
items.content: Systemic therapy is the mainstay of care for metastatic RCC, with molecularly targeted agents being central. These include tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) targeting VEGF receptors and mTOR signaling, as well as checkpoint inhibitors like nivolumab.
keyPoints: Metastatic RCC is refractory to cytotoxic chemotherapy.
Targeted therapies improved outcomes significantly with TKIs and mTOR inhibitors.
Nivolumab demonstrated superior overall survival compared to everolimus in pretreated patients, becoming the new second-line agent of choice.
Combination regimens like nivolumab plus ipilimumab or axitinib plus pembrolizumab showed 40–70% objective radiographic responses and about 10% complete disappearance of cancer.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · METASTATIC DISEASE

items.question: What percentage of malignant neoplasms arising from the kidney are renal cell carcinomas?
items.explanation: Renal cell carcinomas account for 90–95% of malignant neoplasms arising from the kidney.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · Renal Cell Carcinoma | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the male-to-female ratio for renal cell carcinoma incidence in the United States?
items.explanation: The male-to-female ratio for renal cell carcinoma is 2:1.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · Renal Cell Carcinoma | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a risk factor associated with renal cell carcinoma?
items.explanation: Risk factors for renal cell carcinoma include cigarette smoking, obesity, and hypertension.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · Renal Cell Carcinoma | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which chromosomal event is uniformly seen as the earliest in the development of clear cell renal cell carcinoma (RCC)?
items.explanation: Loss of chromosome 3p is the earliest event in clear cell RCC development.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · PATHOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.question: Which gene's inactivation through upregulation of VEGF signaling promotes tumor angiogenesis and growth in clear cell RCC?
items.explanation: Inactivation of the VHL gene leads to increased VEGF signaling, promoting angiogenesis.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · PATHOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.question: Which genetic alteration is associated with certain papillary variants of renal cell carcinoma?
items.explanation: Gain of chromosome 7 (where MET is located) is a hallmark event in certain papillary RCC variants.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · PATHOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.question: Which symptom is most commonly associated with the initial presentation of renal cell carcinoma (RCC)?
options: Hematuria, flank or abdominal pain, and a palpable mass
Fever, weight loss, anemia, and varicocele
items.explanation: Hematuria, flank or abdominal pain, and a palpable mass were part of the classic triad for initial presentation before modern imaging.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What is the most common method by which renal cell carcinoma (RCC) tumors are detected?
options: Incidental finding on a radiograph during evaluation for other conditions
CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis as part of standard evaluation
MRI of the inferior vena cava
items.explanation: Tumors are commonly detected incidentally on a radiograph, especially with widespread use of cross-sectional imaging.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What is the most common site for distant metastases in renal cell carcinoma (RCC)?
items.explanation: The lungs are the most common sites of distant metastases for RCC.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which stage of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is characterized by tumors that extend through the renal capsule but are confined to Gerota's fascia?
items.explanation: Stage III RCC extends through the renal capsule and is confined to Gerota's fascia.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · STAGING AND PROGNOSIS | LOCALIZED TUMOR

items.question: What is the standard management for stage I or II renal cell carcinoma tumors?
items.explanation: The standard management for stage I or II RCC is radical or partial nephrectomy.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · STAGING AND PROGNOSIS | LOCALIZED TUMOR

items.question: Which of the following factors is NOT part of the IMDC risk model for advanced renal cell carcinoma?
items.explanation: Tumor size is not a factor in the IMDC risk model; it includes poor performance status, low hemoglobin, high serum calcium, high neutrophil levels, high platelet levels, and <1-year interval from diagnosis to systemic treatment.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · STAGING AND PROGNOSIS | LOCALIZED TUMOR

items.question: Which treatment is considered for carefully selected patients with stage IV renal cell carcinoma (RCC) despite the presence of metastases?
items.explanation: Cytoreductive nephrectomy is considered for carefully selected patients with stage IV RCC despite the presence of metastases.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · METASTATIC DISEASE

items.question: What type of therapy has become a mainstay in the treatment of metastatic renal cell carcinoma (RCC)?
options: Systemic therapy with molecularly targeted agents
items.explanation: Systemic therapy with molecularly targeted agents is the mainstay of care for metastatic RCC.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · METASTATIC DISEASE

items.question: Which agent demonstrated superior overall survival compared to everolimus in a randomized trial for patients who had progressed on prior TKI therapy?
items.explanation: Nivolumab demonstrated superior overall survival compared to everolimus in a randomized trial for patients who had progressed on prior TKI therapy.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · METASTATIC DISEASE

items.statement: The incidence of cancers of the kidney and renal pelvis has been steadily decreasing in recent years.
items.explanation: The incidence rose for three decades, plateaued, but then increased to approximately 81,000 cases annually.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · Renal Cell Carcinoma | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome is associated with a high risk of developing clear cell renal cell carcinoma.
items.explanation: Individuals with VHL syndrome have a 70% estimated lifetime risk of developing clear cell RCC.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · Renal Cell Carcinoma | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Chromophobe renal cell carcinoma typically originates from the proximal tubules.
items.explanation: Chromophobe RCC originates from the distal nephron, not the proximal tubules.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · PATHOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.statement: Loss of chromosome 9p in renal cell carcinoma is associated with a high risk for early metastatic dissemination.
items.explanation: Early loss of chromosome 9p correlates with poor cancer-specific survival due to increased metastasis.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · PATHOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.statement: Erythrocytosis is noted at presentation in about 50% of patients with renal cell carcinoma.
items.explanation: Erythrocytosis is noted at presentation in only about 3% of patients.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: MRI is useful for evaluating the inferior vena cava when CT is contraindicated due to impaired renal function.
items.explanation: MRI is indeed used in cases where intravenous contrast with CT is prohibited by impaired renal function.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: Radical nephrectomy is contraindicated in stage III renal cell carcinoma.
items.explanation: Radical nephrectomy can be performed for stage III disease, which may include thrombectomy if there's extension into the renal vein or inferior vena cava.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · STAGING AND PROGNOSIS | LOCALIZED TUMOR

items.statement: Pembrolizumab is approved for patients with low risk of recurrence following nephrectomy.
items.explanation: Pembrolizumab is approved for patients with increased risk of recurrence, not those with low risk.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · STAGING AND PROGNOSIS | LOCALIZED TUMOR

items.statement: Radiation therapy is primarily used as the mainstay of care for metastatic renal cell carcinoma.
items.explanation: Systemic therapy with molecularly targeted agents, not radiation therapy, is the mainstay of care for metastatic RCC.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · METASTATIC DISEASE

items.statement: Nivolumab has been positioned as a second-line agent in pretreated patients after demonstrating superior overall survival compared to everolimus.
items.explanation: Nivolumab demonstrated superior overall survival compared to everolimus, positioning it as the new second-line agent for pretreated patients.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · METASTATIC DISEASE

items.assertion: Nephron-sparing surgery may be appropriate for patients with bilateral tumors.
items.reason: Patients with bilateral tumors benefit from nephron-sparing approaches to preserve renal function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why nephron-sparing surgery is considered in such cases.
items.reference: Renal Cell Carcinoma · STAGING AND PROGNOSIS | LOCALIZED TUMOR
